item.title: destroyInstance() leaves SSH private key on disk after instance is destroyed
item.intent: A destroyed instance must leave no credentials on disk — the SSH private key file must be deleted as part of the destroy flow, not left at ~/.clawhq/cloud/keys/<id>.pem forever.
item.description: src/cloud/provisioning/engine.ts destroyInstance() calls removeInstance() to clean the registry but never deletes the SSH private key file. The key is generated at src/cloud/provisioning/ssh-keygen.ts line 46 as deployDir/cloud/keys/<instanceId>.pem (mode 0600). After destroy, this file persists on disk indefinitely. The fix is in engine.ts destroyInstance(): after removeInstance() succeeds, read instance.sshKeyPath and delete the file if it exists. Use fs.rmSync with force:true so a missing file is not an error.
steps: Read src/cloud/provisioning/engine.ts destroyInstance() (lines ~258-295) — confirm removeInstance() is the last call and instance.sshKeyPath is accessible before removal
Read src/cloud/provisioning/ssh-keygen.ts — confirm the key file path pattern: deployDir/cloud/keys/<instanceId>.pem
In destroyInstance(), before calling removeInstance(): capture instance.sshKeyPath (it will be gone from registry after removal)
After removeInstance() returns true: if captured sshKeyPath is set, call fs.rmSync(sshKeyPath, { force: true }) to delete the private key file
Run src/cloud/provisioning tests: registry.test.ts and ssh-keygen.test.ts — confirm no regressions
acceptanceCriteria: After destroyInstance() succeeds, the SSH private key file at instance.sshKeyPath no longer exists on disk
If instance.sshKeyPath was not set (older registry entries), destroy completes without error
DigitalOcean, AWS, GCP, and Hetzner destroy flows are all covered — the fix is in engine.ts (shared path), not in provider adapters
